candidate_problems.text: What exactly does 'sharing the same physical reality' add beyond generic repeated-game assumptions, and can it be formalized as a distinct structural variable? The conjecture treats co-embodiment in one world as creating interdependence, observability, and inescapable future consequences, but it is unclear which of these follow necessarily, which are contingent, and whether some shared worlds instead increase opportunities for decisive predation. A sharper model is needed to distinguish when physical co-presence robustly induces iterated incentives versus when it merely supplies a battlefield.

Where are the phase boundaries between cooperative and destructive regimes in finite-mind systems? The conjecture identifies factors like observability, memory, reputation, time horizon, asymmetry, and decisive dominance, but leaves unresolved how these interact, whether there are threshold effects or tipping points, and which variables are true leverage points. This is a strong open problem because a systems-level map of the transition surface would turn the conjecture from a qualitative intuition into a predictive theory.

How does the conjecture survive under extreme capability asymmetries, especially when one finite mind can cheaply monitor, manipulate, commit, or eliminate others? The text says cooperative pressure collapses when one agent expects decisive dominance, but it remains open whether sufficiently capable agents still preserve others instrumentally for information, resilience, legitimacy, or coordination benefits, or whether domination generically converts the game into a one-shot extraction problem. This is a key unresolved tension because the most consequential real cases may be precisely those with large asymmetries rather than near-peer repeated play.
